Remaining: add 2b26d886... to TR3000 and M3000 peer lists, and add M3000's full id (7faf041c...) to the new node — only its 8-hex prefix is recorded in beads; recover it from M3000 when powered.

title: CRDT peer/endpoint announcement: gossip-propagated reachability for relay-independent cross-site discovery
description: Extend the existing gossip CRDT (already carries DHCP leases, DNS, subnet claims) with a peer-reachability/endpoint entry: each node announces its iroh reachability info (EndpointAddr / direct addrs / relay url) to the mesh, so every node learns how to reach every peer WITHOUT depending on iroh's built-in discovery (mDNS needs same-L2; relay/pkarr needs internet). This is the enabler for OFFLINE, RELAY-INDEPENDENT, CROSS-SITE discovery — the buw endgame. Bootstrap caveat: gossip runs over iroh, so it can't bootstrap the FIRST connection (roster node-ids + iroh discovery do that); the CRDT then propagates the canonical peer/endpoint list mesh-wide, letting a node reach peers it never directly discovered and speeding reconnection. Reserve client-facing DNS/mDNS for resolvable SERVICES only, not infra peer discovery. Emerged from buw.8; duke's CRDT-as-announcement-medium idea 2026-07-01. Design: new CRDT map (node_id -> reachability record, HLC-stamped, FWW like the other maps in crdt/), published on connect/addr-change + anti-entropy; meshd feeds learned addrs into the iroh endpoint / connection manager as dial hints.
design: Reuse crdt/ patterns (subnet/lease/dns maps, HLC merge, sync.rs gossip). New record: { node_id, relay_url: Option, direct_addrs: Vec<SocketAddr>, hlc }. Publish from meshd on endpoint addr-change (iroh exposes a direct-addresses watcher). On receive, add as dial candidates for that node (endpoint.add_node_addr or the connection manager's dial path). Interacts with buw.3 ConnManager. Keep it a HINT layer over iroh discovery, not a replacement for the bootstrap.
acceptance_criteria: A node with NO mDNS (cross-site) and NO relay/internet learns a peer's reachability from the CRDT and establishes an iroh connection using it; peer reachability converges mesh-wide via gossip + anti-entropy.

title: buw: --lan overlay underlay addressing (mjolnir0 owns 10.254.x; how do LAN nodes find each other's underlay?)
description: Found during buw.3/4 meshd integration (buw.7 validation). In per-peer LAN mode meshd assigns the node's backhaul_addr(10.254.x) to the backhaul iface AND pins the iroh socket there so peers dial the fully-derived address (0yb.1, auu). In OVERLAY mode that address belongs to mjolnir0 (the overlay TUN), so meshd now SKIPS the backhaul-iface assignment + socket pin in overlay mode (mjolnir0 carries 10.254.x; the iroh underlay uses its own transport). That makes --overlay --internet clean (underlay = iroh relays/hole-punch, no conflict). OPEN: --overlay --lan needs a DIFFERENT underlay addressing/dialing scheme since the derived-addr-on-backhaul-iface trick is now used by the overlay, not the underlay — e.g. give the underlay iface its own LAN addr + dial peers by mDNS/underlay addr, decoupled from the overlay's 10.254.x. Blocks a fully-local two-node --overlay --lan validation; the fleet validates --overlay --internet instead (spike/buw-overlay-fleet-validate.sh).
design:
DECISION (2026-07-01, per duke): do NOT introduce a second static/derived-IPv4 scheme for the overlay underlay. The derived 10.254.x now lives on the mjolnir0 OVERLAY TUN; the iroh UNDERLAY discovers peers on its own.

UNBLOCKED: MultipathNotNegotiated is NOT an iroh multipath bug — it was an L23/RouterOS-container artifact (duplicate 172.20.0.2 on the shared L2), proven by the auu native retest. Memory iroh-lan-backhaul-findings corrected. So iroh binding wildcard with multiple candidate addresses is fine on clean networks; single-addr pinning is no longer required for multipath reasons.

APPROACH:
1) Underlay discovery = iroh native: mDNS by bare node id on LAN; relay/pkarr on internet. No derived-addr pin. (The meshd main() socket-pin + assign_backhaul_addr(backhaul_iface) is per-peer-LAN-only and already skipped in overlay mode.)
2) Peer announcement = CRDT (PREFERRED): the existing gossip CRDT already carries DHCP leases / DNS / subnet claims across the mesh — extend it with a peer-reachability/endpoint entry so nodes announce themselves to the canonical mesh list. This maintains/enriches peer discovery without a second IP scheme. Reserve client-facing DNS/mDNS for resolvable SERVICES only, not infra peer discovery.
   - Bootstrap caveat (chicken-and-egg): gossip runs OVER iroh, so the CRDT can't bootstrap the FIRST connection. Roster node-ids + iroh discovery (mDNS/relay) bootstrap; the CRDT then maintains the canonical peer/endpoint list and speeds reconnection / relay-independence thereafter.

CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: iroh must NOT advertise mjolnir0's 10.254.x as an underlay candidate — connecting to a peer via their overlay addr would route iroh-over-overlay-over-iroh (loop). Bind iroh to the underlay iface(s) or filter the overlay TUN addr out of iroh's candidate set. Verify mjolnir0 (created after bind) doesn't get re-scanned into candidates.

DOC FIX: meshd.rs main() socket-pin comment still blames MultipathNotNegotiated as an iroh bug — reword to the L23-artifact finding (keep the pin for the per-peer LAN path's deterministic dialing, 0yb.1, but drop the multipath justification).

ACCEPTANCE unchanged: two --overlay --lan nodes on a shared L2 form an iroh connection and carry client traffic over mjolnir0, underlay addressed distinctly from the overlay's 10.254.x (via iroh discovery + CRDT announcement, not a second static scheme).
acceptance_criteria: Two --overlay --lan nodes on a shared L2 form an iroh connection and carry client traffic over mjolnir0, with the underlay addressed distinctly from the overlay's 10.254.x.
notes: IMPL DECISION (2026-07-01): iroh 1.0 ADVERTISES mjolnir0 10.254.x (no TUN/private filter in netwatch ip.rs) and has NO public API to exclude a discovered addr (patching the netwatch dep is out). Fix in OUR code instead: the overlay router DROPS 10.254.0.0/16 unicast (returns None) — those are the mjolnir0 interface/neighbour addresses, never a forwarded data path (babel control = fe80::; client data routes by CLIENT dest via FIB, so the 10.254.x next-hop is resolved by the FIB entry, never a packet dest — true for single- and multi-hop). Dropping them means iroh probes to a peer overlay addr get dropped by the overlay -> no bogus iroh-over-overlay path forms (which MultipathNotNegotiated no longer makes fatal anyway, but the tunneled path is a latent fragility worth preventing). Underlay discovery = iroh native (mDNS/relay), already used in overlay mode (bind=None). No second static IP scheme. meshd socket-pin comment doc-fixed.
close_reason: DONE + validated locally 2026-07-01. iroh 1.0 advertises the mjolnir0 10.254.x (no TUN/private filter in netwatch, no public exclude API) so the fix is in our code: overlay router DROPS 10.254.0.0/16 unicast (never a data path — babel control is fe80::, client data routes by client dest via FIB) which stops iroh forming a bogus iroh-over-overlay path; overlay dials peers by NODE ID + iroh native discovery (mDNS/relay), no derived-addr pin; underlay addressed independently of the overlay. VALIDATED: two meshd --overlay --lan nodes in two netns over a veth formed a DIRECT iroh connection (kind=DIRECT paths=1, remote=underlay 10.9.0.x NOT overlay 10.254.x, no relay), both logged 'overlay peer connected' (connection manager registered bidirectionally), each with one mjolnir0 (10.254.180.230 / 10.254.170.105). Harness: spike/buw-overlay-2node.sh. No second static/derived IPv4 scheme (per duke). Memory iroh-lan-backhaul-findings corrected re: MultipathNotNegotiated=L23 artifact; meshd socket-pin comment doc-fixed.

title: Concurrent AP + 802.11s mesh-point on same radio renders start_disabled=1 (mt76/MT7981)
description:
Discovered while hardware-verifying mjolnir-mesh-bnd (802.11r FT) on a Cudy TR3000 v1
(MT7981, OpenWrt 25.12.4, wpad-mesh-mbedtls) at 192.168.1.1: adding a second wifi-iface
in AP mode on radio0 (2.4GHz) alongside the existing 802.11s mesh-point (wireless.meshbh,
also on radio0) causes hostapd to render start_disabled=1 for the AP bss, and it never
broadcasts an SSID (confirmed via `iw dev` showing the interface present but no ssid line,
and grep of the rendered /var/run/hostapd-phy0.conf). `iw phy` reports generic combo
support for 16x AP+mesh, so this is a driver/ordering quirk, not a hard capability limit.

Reproduced with FT config options completely stripped out of the test iface too, so it's
NOT related to 802.11r/FT — it's a plain concurrent-AP-on-mesh-radio issue.

This directly affects mjolnir-mesh-ab4 (the existing CLIENT_AP_2G feature in
setup-wireless.sh, which puts a client AP concurrent with the mesh-point on radio0
specifically for 2.4-only IoT/ESP32 devices) — on this hardware/driver combo, that AP may
never actually come up. Needs investigation: does it eventually self-clear (a channel
switch / CSA event to un-disable it), does a different bring-up ordering avoid it (e.g.
start the AP before the mesh-point), or is this a known mt76 limitation requiring a
workaround (separate radio only, or a delayed re-enable hotplug hook like the existing
30-mesh-powersave one in setup-wireless.sh)?
notes: THIRD MODEL DATA POINT (2026-07-01, m3000-b/2b26 bring-up): Cudy M3000 with a LIVE concurrent clientap2g behaves like the WR3000S, NOT the TR3000 — the 802.11s mesh-point never joins at all (box was off-air since provisioning). Recovery procedure now confirmed on both affected models: disable/remove the 2.4 AP (current setup-wireless renders it disabled) + REBOOT (wifi reload is NOT enough — wpad's stale phy0 claim survives it). Per-model summary: WR3000S=mesh blocked, M3000=mesh blocked, TR3000=mesh survives but AP start_disabled. Staged-disabled sections are inert on all three (fleet-wide since b9eb4f8).

title: buw deploy: install-node.sh must apk add kmod-tun (/dev/net/tun ENOENT on first retest)
description: qz9 deploy finding: first retest hit /dev/net/tun ENOENT because kmod-tun was absent. deploy/openwrt/install-node.sh must `apk add kmod-tun` (opkg on 23.x) so mjolnir0 can be created on the Cudy/OpenWrt boxes.
acceptance_criteria: Fresh OpenWrt node has /dev/net/tun present after install-node.sh.
close_reason: Already implemented in concurrent openwrt deploy work (install-node.sh:82-87,123-125): pm_install kmod-tun via the apk/opkg PM_HELPERS abstraction, guarded by KMOD_TUN_OK (non-fatal), idempotent, with a clear warning if it fails. bash -n passes. buw.1 finding addressed.

title: 802.11r fast-transition config (cheap)
description: TIER: mid-tier agent — OpenWrt UCI + on-hardware test. Part of jfp. Configure 802.11r FT on the client APs: ieee80211r=1, mobility_domain, ft_over_ds, R0KH/R1KH as needed; FT-SAE (5GHz WPA3 AP) + FT-PSK (2.4 WPA2 IoT AP, ab4). VERIFY the shipped wpad variant (wpad-mesh-mbedtls swap in install-node.sh) also carries AP-side FT — minimal wpad builds drop it. Test: client roams between two APs, measure re-assoc time (ms, not a full handshake). In the flat-L2 island default this alone gives seamless roaming.
notes:
HARDWARE-VERIFIED 2026-07-01 on real node (Cudy TR3000 v1 / MT7981, OpenWrt 25.12.4,
wpad-mesh-mbedtls, SSH 192.168.1.1 via wired br-lan/eth1 — deployed via a prior session,
config diverged from setup-wireless.sh's current template: uses network='client'/br-client
not 'lan', custom SSID). Applied FT config to THROWAWAY test wifi-iface sections (not the
live 'clientap'/'meshbh'), reloaded, inspected the actual rendered hostapd-phy*.conf and
`iw dev`/`ubus call network.wireless status`, then fully removed the test sections and
diffed `uci show wireless` byte-for-byte back to the pre-test baseline — confirmed clean,
meshd still running, peer still configured, no residue.

Results — everything previously flagged as needing hardware is now CONFIRMED:
  - wpad binary (/usr/sbin/wpad) contains FT-PSK/FT-SAE/r0kh strings directly — wpad-mesh-mbedtls
    does carry AP-side FT, not just inferred from the Makefile.
  - r0kh/r1kh UCI value IS space-separated, matching what's implemented (rendered exactly as
    `r0kh=ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff * <key>` / `r1kh=00:00:00:00:00:00 00:00:00:00:00:00 <key>` in
    /var/run/hostapd-phy1.conf) — the comma-separated format a third-party tool suggested was wrong.
  - encryption='sae-mixed' + ieee80211r=1 correctly renders
    wpa_key_mgmt=SAE FT-SAE WPA-PSK WPA-PSK-SHA256 FT-PSK — FT-SAE is really in there.
  - ft_psk_generate_local=1 on psk2 correctly renders wpa_key_mgmt=WPA-PSK FT-PSK.
  - hostapd starts cleanly with all of the above, no config-rejection errors in logread.

STILL OPEN — the actual acceptance criterion ("client roams between two APs, measure
re-assoc time") needs a SECOND node reachable at the same time plus a real FT-capable
client device; only one node is connected right now. Keeping this issue open until that
two-node roam test happens; the config-generation risk is now retired.

Separate finding (NOT this issue, filing standalone): concurrent AP + 802.11s mesh-point
on the SAME radio (radio0/2.4GHz, mt76/MT7981) renders start_disabled=1 in hostapd's
generated config and the AP never broadcasts (confirmed with FT options stripped out too,
so it's unrelated to FT) — this affects the existing CLIENT_AP_2G feature (mjolnir-mesh-ab4)
in general on this hardware, not just FT-PSK testing.

title: Widen base pool to /8 configurable — the cheap 256->65k node lift
description: TIER: mid-tier agent — mechanical + tests. Part of s2i. Make the client supernet configurable (read from CRDT root / config), default 10.0.0.0/8 (65536 /24s). Thread through alloc.rs DEFAULT_MESH_SPACE + the exhaustion path; update tests (the 256-cap exhaustion test alloc.rs:253 becomes 65536). No allocation-algorithm change — same pick_subnet, bigger space.

title: L3 roaming: /32-over-babel + CRDT-lease IP continuity (max intelligence)
description: TIER: max intelligence (Opus) + verify — distributed state + routing correctness with races. Part of jfp; only needed in the L3-per-hop mode (per-node /24, mesh_fwding=0, or cross-site). On roam the new node consults the gossiped CRDT lease (MAC->IP reservation), offers the SAME IP, advertises a /32 into babel while the old node withdraws its /32 — traffic follows the device, TCP survives. Hard parts: device flapping between APs (dueling /32 advertisements), withdraw timing, consistency with the lease CRDT. DEPENDS on i8p (CRDT lease sync).

title: Anti-entropy pt2: pull-based digest / range reconciliation (max intelligence)
description: TIER: max intelligence (Opus) + adversarial verify — distributed-consistency protocol, classic looks-right-is-wrong. Part 2 of 5r0. Periodically pick a random peer and exchange a COMPACT DIGEST (per-key HLC-max / version summary, or range/Merkle set reconciliation a la Scuttlebutt/Cassandra/Willow); peer sends only the delta. The bandwidth-scalable reconciliation needed once leases/DNS/services (O(M)) are wired and full re-broadcast (pt1) stops being cheap. Must be provably convergent under concurrent updates, partitions, reordering; verify with an adversarial harness (inject drops/dupes/partitions, assert convergence). Do pt1 first.

title: Anti-entropy pt1: periodic full-state re-broadcast + disk persistence (cheap)
description: TIER: mid-tier agent, mechanical (review the interval + idempotence). Part 1 of 5r0. (a) Every 15-30s each node re-broadcasts its FULL known claim map over the existing iroh-gossip topic — not just its own claim (that is the weaker nrh). State is tiny (~64KB at the 256-node cap), so it is cheap and fixes late-joiner / dropped-packet / restart convergence. Subsumes nrh. (b) Persist the CRDT store to disk (the in-RAM Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> at meshd:480) so a rebooting node serves DHCP/DNS immediately and seeds reconciliation. No new protocol design — a timer + load/save.
notes: Implemented: (a) anti_entropy_loop in mjolnir-meshd.rs re-broadcasts the full ClaimStore over gossip every 20s (const ANTI_ENTROPY_INTERVAL), subsuming nrh. (b) load_claims/persist_claims postcard-encode the store to --claims-file (default /etc/mjolnir/claims.state; temp-file+rename for crash safety), loaded on startup and rewritten each anti-entropy cycle. 6 new unit tests + smoke-tested a real node: claim published -> anti-entropy re-broadcast+persisted -> restart restores from disk. Commit cf07581 (unrelated pre-existing panic-hook fix split out into 9066bc3).
close_reason: anti-entropy re-broadcast + disk persistence implemented, tested, committed

title: Seamless roaming: 802.11r fast-transition + /32-over-babel + CRDT-lease IP continuity
description: Roaming has TWO independent layers, both required for seamless handoff. (1) FAST RADIO HANDOFF = 802.11r (Fast BSS Transition): pre-computes keys so re-association is milliseconds instead of a full 4-way/EAP handshake — the part that matters for VoIP handoff. Works both bands; FT-SAE (WPA3) + FT-PSK (WPA2); needs full wpad with FT + mobility_domain/ft_over_ds config (verify the wpad-mesh variant also carries AP-side FT). (2) IP CONTINUITY across L3. MODE-DEPENDENT: in the flat-L2 island DEFAULT (one SSID/subnet, mesh_fwding=1) 802.11r ALONE is fully seamless — client keeps its IP on the shared subnet; use this for dense client roaming / events. In the L3-per-hop mode (per-node /24, mesh_fwding=0, or cross-site) 802.11r is NECESSARY-NOT-SUFFICIENT: the client lands on a new subnet, so pair it with /32-host-route-over-babel + the gossiped CRDT lease (the new node holds the MAC->IP reservation, offers the SAME IP, advertises a /32 so traffic follows the device). The two are COMPLEMENTARY layers, not alternatives. Documented in network-architecture.md (Roaming Across Sites -> Fast roaming (802.11r) and the L2/L3 split). RELATES: i8p (CRDT lease sync = the same-IP offer), 0yb (the mesh_fwding=0 L3 mode where /32 roaming becomes required), bvw (client AP/bridge). Answers the roaming worry: yes, 802.11r works — flat-island default is fully seamless with it alone; L3 mode needs the /32+CRDT pairing on top.

title: Widen client address space beyond /16 (configurable base, /8 default) + IPv6 path — lift the 256-node ceiling
description: Default supernet 10.42.0.0/16 at /24-per-node = max 256 nodes / ~65k clients (alloc.rs DEFAULT_MESH_SPACE + CLIENT_PREFIX_LEN; exhaustion test alloc.rs:253). Insufficient for the stated hundreds-of-thousands of users (100k users ~= 400 nodes at 254/node — already past /16). NEAR-TERM (cheap, before a real fleet): make the base pool configurable and default it to 10.0.0.0/8 (65536 /24s = 65k nodes, ~16M clients). This is yau item B (pool-widen knob) elevated to a concrete task. LONG-TERM (real answer for global scale): IPv6 — the data model is already version-agnostic (LeaseEntry.ip is std::net::IpAddr, prior-art 3.3), so v6 is additive; a node-id-derived v6 prefix also removes the allocation race entirely (see 0vc, IPv6-from-node_id). BEYOND /8 / global: hierarchy/regionalisation (per-region supernets + inter-region routing) — the flat single-space model caps out. RELATES: yau (governance of the commons as it fills), 5r0 (bigger space + more nodes = more CRDT state -> anti-entropy + sharding matter more), 99f (stale-claim reclamation frees slots). TENSION: a bigger /24-per-node space is free for babel (still 1 route/node) but grows gossip/CRDT state — pair with 5r0.

title: U1 (purist): client AP as ingress onto the iroh L3 overlay — e2ee from the edge
description: Follow-up to buw. buw default (U2) keeps same-site traffic on the native 802.11s L2 — fast, but only per-hop SAE (intermediate mesh routers see plaintext). U1 routes same-site over the overlay TUN (mjolnir0) too, so the client AP becomes the INGRESS where plaintext client traffic enters the encrypted iroh L3; from there it is e2ee QUIC all the way to the egress node — even intermediate forwarding routers cannot read it. Plaintext exists only on the client<->AP radio link (WPA2/SAE) and at the egress node. This is the confidentiality property documented in network-architecture.md Security (End-to-end vs per-hop confidentiality). COST: QUIC encap on local radio hops = CPU + airtime on the very link babel could use natively; gated by the iroh-use-policy CPU/efficiency concern. So it is opt-in / per-deployment, NOT the default — likely a mode flag (like lan_tunnels) selecting overlay-for-same-site. DEPENDS on buw (needs the single overlay TUN + connection manager first). Build the U2 default first; this is the purist follow-up duke wants as an option.

title: Gossip anti-entropy: re-broadcast known state + pull-based digest reconciliation (late joiners, drops, restarts)
description: The CRDT gossip is publish-once best-effort with NO anti-entropy, so a late joiner, a dropped packet, or a restarted node never reconciles (the store is in-RAM and empty on boot). nrh proposes the minimal fix (periodically re-publish your OWN claim). This bead is the full version: (1) periodically re-broadcast the full small known state (not just your own facts) — cheap at current scale, ~64KB at the 256-node ceiling — which subsumes nrh and fixes most cases; (2) pull-based anti-entropy: periodically pick a random peer and exchange a compact DIGEST (per-key HLC-max / version summary, or range/Merkle set reconciliation a la Scuttlebutt/Cassandra/Willow), peer sends only what is missing — this is the bandwidth-scalable answer once leases/DNS/services (O(M)) are wired; (3) persist CRDT state to disk so a rebooting node serves DHCP/DNS immediately and seeds reconciliation (see p2p-resilience Phase 5; relates to mab CRDT-persistence). Note: hashgraph-style consensus is overkill — we need eventual convergence, not BFT total order; take the keep-gossiping-what-you-heard intuition, drop the voting DAG. Supersedes/contains nrh.

title: HLC counter never advances (now_hlc always counter=0) — FWW degenerates to lowest-wall-clock-wins, clock-skew-sensitive
description: now_hlc in mjolnir-meshd.rs stamps raw SystemTime with counter:0 always; nothing increments the counter on equal wall-clocks or advances to max(local,received)+1 on receive. So the Hybrid Logical Clock is really just a physical timestamp + node_id tiebreak, and first-writer-wins becomes lowest-wall-clock-wins — a node with a skewed clock systematically wins or loses subnet claims. Still convergent, but the winner may not be the real first claimer (bad on cheap routers with no RTC/NTP). The new gossip-and-crdt.md even documents the counter as advancing, so this is also a doc/impl mismatch. Fix: implement real HLC update on send and receive (advance counter on wall-clock ties, take max on receive).

title: Internet egress: gateway redistributes a default route into babel (multi-egress nearest-exit + NAT)
description: No node advertises a default route into the mesh today, so mesh clients have no internet egress (the --internet flag is only an iroh discovery/relay mode, not L3 egress). Design: a node flagged as a gateway detects its WAN default route, redistributes 0.0.0.0/0 into babeld with a metric, and masquerades mesh->WAN. babel then gives nearest-exit and multi-homing across MULTIPLE gateways for free via its metric/feasibility selection, and reconverges when a gateway loses WAN. Non-trivial bits: (a) a gateway role flag in config; (b) the redistribute filter must ALLOW the default from the gateway while still denying stray WAN prefixes — intersects the WAN-leak filter work in ctr; (c) NAT/masquerade + firewall (note mesh is currently folded into the lan zone, which already permits lan->wan); (d) avoid blackholing when a gateway loses its uplink (withdraw the route). Related: ctr, and the firewall-zone hardening bead.

title: Concurrent 2.4 GHz client AP alongside the 802.11s mesh-point (2.4-only IoT/ESP32 access)
description: Today setup-wireless.sh puts the ONLY client AP on 5 GHz; 2.4 GHz is mesh-backhaul-only. So 2.4-GHz-only devices (most ESP32 variants, much cheap IoT) cannot join the network at all. Add a concurrent AP vif on the 2.4 GHz radio (mt76 supports mesh-point + AP on one phy/channel) so 2.4-only clients can associate. Default WPA2-PSK (psk2) for ESP32 compatibility (many lack WPA3-SAE), same SSID as the 5 GHz AP so devices roam across bands on one L2. Tradeoff: the 2.4 AP shares channel 6 + airtime with the backhaul (fine for low-bandwidth IoT; heavy clients should prefer 5 GHz). NEEDS on-hardware verification that concurrent mesh+AP comes up on the MT7981 and that an ESP32 associates + gets a mesh /24 lease. Related: kso (radio split), w1l (Cudy fleet), bvw (br-client/dnsmasq productionization — the 2.4 AP should land on whatever client bridge bvw settles on).
notes: DECISION 2026-07-01 (with dorje): setup-wireless.sh now renders wireless.clientap2g UNCONDITIONALLY but disabled; CLIENT_AP_2G=1 is the explicit enable flag (sets disabled=0), field enable = 'uci set wireless.clientap2g.disabled=0; uci commit wireless; wifi reload'. Rationale: keep the 2.4 backhaul clean (oaq breaks mesh join on WR3000S when the AP is LIVE; and even post-oaq the AP taxes backhaul airtime — enable deliberately on leaf/IoT nodes only). 5 GHz alone doesn't reach the target coverage, so 2.4 client access stays on the roadmap = this bead + oaq. VERIFIED on all three fleet models (WR3000S in-band over the mesh, TR3000, M3000): the disabled section creates no vif, mesh joins/peers fine, health gates passed.

title: Local HTTP-to-Iroh gateway on OpenWrt for non-iroh clients (offline-capable)
description: PROBLEM: L3-native clients (apps with iroh built in) do their own e2e and offload crypto from the router; but most users hit a mesh service with a plain browser (http://service.mesh) and know nothing about node-ids/tunnels. They need a gateway. The EXISTING Mjolnir gateway (docs/vision/mjolnir-integration.md: vm.worldtree.network) is a CENTRAL, cloud-fronted HTTP-to-Iroh bridge (Browser->Cloudflare->Gateway server->Iroh QUIC->VM, z32-NodeId subdomain, HTTP/WS/SSE) -- great for internet reach, but needs internet on both ends + a central server, so it is NOT usable at an offline DWEB event. NEED an on-node OpenWrt option. TWO sub-cases: (a) target has a routable MESH IP (service on a claimed /24 or router-hosted) -> .mesh DNS + babel/iroh-tunnel IP routing already handles it; mostly free once service discovery (xqn) lands -- covers MOST event cases. (b) target is IROH-ONLY (NodeId-addressed: remote VM, roaming device w/o stable mesh IP, cross-internet service) -> need a LOCAL HTTP-to-Iroh bridge daemon on the gateway node (port the Mjolnir gateway translation logic, minus Cloudflare/public-CA). CONSTRAINTS on OpenWrt: weak MT7981 CPU -> the gateway node bears iroh crypto for ALL its dumb clients, so make it opt-in per designated node, not every leaf; local TLS story (browsers want https; .mesh has no public CA -> http-only or a local/captive CA); WebTransport-over-iroh (mjolnir-moq / web-transport-iroh) covers browser WEB-APPS that use the WT JS API but NOT plain-URL browsing. SECURITY CAVEAT: for a dumb client, e2e starts at the GATEWAY, not the device -- the client<->gateway last hop is only as private as the local LAN/SAE wifi; true device-to-service e2e requires an iroh-native client. Relates: xqn (service discovery / .mesh responder), iroh-use-policy memory.

title: [tracker] Service discovery pieces still to build (CRDT DNS/Service/Lease -> client-facing)
description: CRDT service-discovery substrate is DEAD SCHEMA today: DnsEntry/ServiceEntry/LeaseEntry (crdt/{dns,service,lease}.rs) + their GossipMessage variants are defined but UNWIRED -- apply loop only handles subnet claims (meshd:700-733, _ => false), nothing publishes them. TO BUILD: (1) publish+apply DnsUpdate/ServiceUpdate/LeaseUpdate over the existing iroh-gossip CRDT; (2) DHCP lease -> CRDT sync (see i8p); (3) client-facing responder/bridge so a phone/laptop query is answered mesh-wide -- a .mesh DNS responder fed by the CRDT and/or an mDNS reflector across segments (today only native .local within one flat island works); (4) the .mesh domain + naming scheme; (5) cross-segment service propagation for the heterogeneous fleet (no universal flat L2). Relates: i8p, ti0, 0yb (node discovery is the prerequisite).

title: Test 802.11s backhaul channel width under speed/load testing
description: When load/speed-testing the mesh, A/B the backhaul channel width and pick what holds rate at the WORST real link (foliage/range), since backhaul is single-channel + single-width mesh-wide. 2.4 GHz: HT20 (cleaner, better SNR margin, foliage) vs HT40 (2x PHY, one 40MHz fits in 2.4). If 5 GHz backhaul is ever trialed: note mt76/802.11s silently falls back to VHT80 even when HE160 is requested (OpenWrt #13880), so the realistic ceiling is VHT80, not HE160. Capture throughput + plink stability per width. Relates to kso (radio config), feeds 2j6 (4-node bench) + w1l.

title: OpenWrt spike scaffolding: status subcommand, procd init+UCI, ipk package
description: Native OpenWrt mt76 spike enablement. (1) mjolnir-meshd status: one-shot ground-truth dump (build stamp, node id, derived backhaul addr, interface addrs, kernel mesh routes incl nexthop) so diagnosis doesn't require log greps (the auu session pain). (2) procd init script + UCI config so meshd is a supervised OpenWrt service. (3) OpenWrt package (Makefile + .ipk) so N nodes flash identically, verified by the build stamp. Lessons from auu: native Linux removes the container dual-address/placeholder trap; build observability in from the start.
notes:
RECONCILED with concurrent OpenWrt work on origin/main (was 8 commits behind when I went to push). The remote already had: procd init for meshd+babeld (m8t), /etc/config/mjolnir UCI, stamped build.sh with dubious-ownership fix + isolated cross-target (0xu), install-node.sh (scp-based), setup-wireless.sh, e4r client-subnet wiring. My drafted procd/UCI/build.sh DUPLICATED that more-mature work, so DROPPED them. Kept only the genuinely-new piece: the status subcommand (origin/main had none), re-applied cleanly onto the new meshd.rs, wired in as service mjolnir-meshd diag, plus a README diagnostics note. Committed 062f82b; host+aarch64 builds pass; status run-verified on host.

OPEN DECISION: ipk packaging. The remote deliberately uses scp-based install-node.sh instead of an ipk. A parallel ipk path (package the canonical files/etc layout, version==build stamp) is still a flash-N-identically win but overlaps install-node.sh. Deferred to user: add ipk aligned to the files/etc layout, or is install-node.sh sufficient?

title: Productionize br-client split + dnsmasq-on-claimed-/24 into deploy scripts
description: The client test required manual uci on the box: a dedicated br-client bridge (AP attached, separate from mgmt br-lan), the claimed /24's .1 on br-client, and a dnsmasq dhcp-range on it. Currently client_iface defaults to br-lan and the /24 must be proto-static for dnsmasq to see it (meshd's netlink-assigned addr is invisible to netifd/dnsmasq, and network reloads wipe it). Bake the br-client + dnsmasq + firewall-zone setup into setup-wireless.sh / a client-setup script, and decide the meshd-vs-netifd ownership of the client+backhaul addresses (meshd should re-assert addrs on netlink events, or netifd should own deterministic per-node addrs).

title: Phase 4 (deferred): dnsmasq DHCP/DNS CRDT sync — daemon glue
description: DEFERRED past the connectivity-first milestone (user decision 2026-06-28). Implement the designed-but-unbuilt daemon glue from docs/network-coordination/dhcp-crdt.md + dnsmasq-integration.md: dhcp-event subcommand (thin Unix-socket client) -> daemon socket /run/mjolnir-mesh.sock -> reservations writer /tmp/mjolnir/reservations (dhcp-hostsfile) + .mesh DNS hosts /tmp/mjolnir/dns (addn-hosts) -> SIGHUP dnsmasq -> LeaseUpdate/DnsUpdate gossip+merge (today apply_subnet_message ignores Lease/DNS msgs) -> FWW conflict deauth via hostapd_cli. Wire dnsmasq via uci (dhcphostsfile/addnhosts/dhcp_script, domain=mesh). Gives mesh-wide synchronized DHCP + roaming + .mesh DNS. CRDT types (LeaseEntry/DnsEntry/ServiceEntry) already exist; this is the wiring lane.

title: Build OpenWrt initramfs for MikroTik L23UGSR (qualcommax/ipq50xx port)
description: Stand up an OpenWrt main tree, integrate the draft L23UGSR-5HaxD2HaxD device port (deploy/openwrt/l23-port/: DTS + device-recipe.mk + 5 companion edits + stub ipq-wifi BDF), and build an initramfs-kernel.bin to netboot the box over BOOTP/TFTP. First boot goal: resolve AArch64-vs-AArch32 handoff and bring up ethernet+SSH for Phase-B recon. Box live at 192.168.0.134 (REST admin:lab); host on its LAN via eno1. No serial yet (header pitch too fine for breadboard headers).
notes:
SERIAL HEADER RECON (fills B8 / DTS serial_0_pins): 10-pin SPI+UART header is 1.8V TTL. GND=pins 2&10; 1.8V rail=pin4; 3.3V rail=pin8; SPI flash data=pin3; UART TX=pin5 (1.8V idle-high); UART RX=pin6; pin7~0.73V float. Console = 1.8V => 3.3V adapter reads garbage; need 1.8V adapter or level shifter to read pin5. See bd memory 'mikrotik-l23ugsr-ipq5018-serial-uart-console-is-1'. Next: read pin5 at 1.8V to capture RouterBOOT banner -> resolves the #1 unknown (AArch64 vs AArch32 handoff).
Triage 2026-06-30: moved in_progress -> open. Not actively being worked (no activity since last update); was mislabeled in_progress. Left claimable in bd ready. Note: blocker 2zz (babeld SIGHUP) is now closed, so apo/data-path validation is unblocked.

title: Mesh: 802.11s + babeld + meshd on booted L23 node
description: 5G 802.11s backhaul + babeld + aarch64 mjolnir-meshd (deploy/openwrt ready). Validate vs mesh-bringup checklist.

title: Bring-up: serial netboot to ethernet+ath11k+radios up
description: TFTP L23 initramfs; debug over serial to console+eth+ath11k+both radios. Tune for 256MB RAM.

title: Netboot infra: dnsmasq BOOTP/TFTP + RouterBOOT etherboot
description: dnsmasq on .164/.165 serving initramfs ELF; RouterBOOT boot-device=ethernet. Non-destructive.

title: meshctl: deploy the mesh/--lan/shared-L2 workflow (not just tun-listen/tun-connect)
description: meshctl deploy still drives the ORIGINAL single-tunnel flow (cmd=tun-listen / tun-connect <blob>, container-net.rsc NAT egress, listener/connector roles in routers.toml). The deployed mesh has since moved to: cmd='mesh --peer <ids>' (defaults to --lan offline), container bridged onto a shared L2 via container-net-lan.rsc, meshd self-assigning a derived 10.254.0.0/16 backhaul, and direct iroh paths (mjolnir-mesh-4pk). That bring-up is currently driven by shell helpers (deploy/mikrotik/deploy-mesh.sh + apply-routing-and-test.sh) and documented in docs/deploy/mesh-bringup.md. Fold it into meshctl: a 'mesh' deploy path that applies container-net-lan.rsc (with the operator's backhaul port), sets IROH_SECRET, runs the container in mesh --lan mode with the peer-id roster (meshctl already derives node ids from secrets via deploy::node_id), and verifies the DIRECT path / claims from the logs. Also update routers.toml away from listener/connector roles. Durable home for the orchestration the shell scripts stopgap.

title: Procure + bench-validate OpenWrt mesh hardware (mt76/ath9k)
description: Research done in docs/research/openwrt-mesh-hardware/. Next: buy 2x OpenWrt One ($89, mt76, first-class OpenWrt support) to validate the full 802.11s + wpad-mesh-mbedtls + babeld stack end-to-end before fleet commit. Key driver verdict: mt76 (mt7915/mt7916/mt7986) and ath9k are SAFE for 802.11s; ath10k needs non-CT firmware; AVOID ath11k/ath12k/wifi-qcom (the MikroTik failure). Recommended fleet board: Banana Pi BPI-R3 (mt76, bare board, U.FL, mPCIe+M.2 for tri-radio). Validate 5GHz mesh-point reliability at a pinned snapshot (watch mt76 #912). Keep backhaul on fixed non-DFS 5GHz channel; dedicate a radio to backhaul.
notes:
2026-06-24 pass-3 (budget/stock): BPI-R3 Mini confirmed scarce ($180-200+, OOS at reputable resellers) — do NOT wait for it. Radio capability identical across mt7981/mt7986 family (same mt76 driver + same mesh bug-tail), so don't pay bare-board premium. BUY PICKS: GL.iNet Flint 2 GL-MT6000 (~$140-160, mt7986, SAME silicon as BPI-R3, mature/stable OpenWrt, in stock = the real replacement); Cudy WR3000 (~$35, mt7981, web-UI flash, cheapest fleet node); GL.iNet GL-A1300 (~$65, ath10k, REMOVABLE RP-SMA antennas) or used Netgear R7800 (~$70, 4x removable) when high-gain antennas needed. GL.iNet GUI mesh is proprietary NOT 802.11s — use LuCI (swap wpad-mesh-openssl) or flash vanilla. See docs/research/openwrt-mesh-hardware/pass3-budget/synthesis-budget.md
Procured (2026-06-28): Cudy WR3000 v1, TR3000, M3000 — all MT7981B/Filogic 820. Bench-validate folds into epic mjolnir-mesh-w1l.

title: Wireless backhaul: replace the wired switch with a WiFi mesh between the 4 nodes
description: Make the 4 nodes self-contained — no home router / no switch. Configure a transparent bridged L2 backhaul over WiFi between the 4 MikroTik L23UGSR (IPQ-5010, RouterOS v7 'wifi' package, dual-band): dedicated backhaul radio + client radio. The container bridge (br-mesh) extends across the WiFi backhaul, so the only change vs wired is container-net-lan.rsc $meshLink -> the WiFi backhaul interface. CRITICAL RISK: L2 multicast must survive the WiFi backhaul (mDNS discovery + ND/ARP depend on it). Depends on the 4-node wired validation passing first (isolate the radio variable).
notes:
DECISION (2026-06-24): NEW nodes = WHOLE OpenWrt mt76 boards (OpenWrt One ~$89 / GL-MT3000 ~$80, USB-C, self-enclosed) running real 802.11s mesh + client AP + the overlay NATIVELY. Existing MikroTiks kept as a secondary AP/STA segment (wifi-qcom can't do 802.11s); interop between the AP/STA segment and the 802.11s island is a KNOWN, ACCEPTED cost (gateway node + L3 routing). Guiding principle: the L3 overlay (iroh+babeld+CRDT) is the product and must run on HETEROGENEOUS hardware; the radio/L2 layer is per-node. Dropped the 'AP/STA-everywhere' idea (single-uplink fragile under mobility/churn) and the 'tiny-bridge-wired-to-MikroTik' idea (if the board is capable enough to bridge, it's capable enough to be the whole node). See docs/network-coordination/radio-backhaul-and-discovery.md. This bead now = stand up the mt76/802.11s island + the AP/STA<->802.11s interop gateway; depends on the aarch64 overlay build and on 2j6.
Wireless backhaul on the Cudy fleet = 2.4 GHz 802.11s mesh-point (decision 2026-06-28). Tracked under epic mjolnir-mesh-w1l / issue mjolnir-mesh-kso.

title: meshd: periodically re-publish subnet claim for gossip convergence
description: HARDWARE FINDING (apo Tier-2): no 'received peer subnet claim' on either router — gossip never converged. Cause: each node publishes its claim ONCE right after the 8s warmup; gossip is best-effort with no anti-entropy, so if the peer's gossip neighbor isn't up at that instant (it usually isn't — both are still forming the swarm), the claim is lost forever. Fix: claim_manager should re-publish the local claim periodically (e.g. every 15-30s) and/or on gossip NeighborUp, so late/reconnecting peers converge. (Note: gossip convergence is for /24 COLLISION avoidance; babeld routing is independent, so this doesn't block routing when ids hash to distinct /24s — but it's needed for correctness.)
notes: Narrow first increment of 5r0 (full anti-entropy: re-broadcast all known state + pull-based digest reconciliation + persistence). Do nrh first if a quick fix is needed; 5r0 subsumes it.

title: validate: wifi client on A reaches a host on B's LAN across the mesh
description: Replace the /31 UDP probe as the success signal with a REAL end-to-end test on hardware (routers .181/.113): a wifi/LAN client on router A pings/connects a host on router B's LAN, traffic crossing the mesh via babeld-installed routes over the iroh TUN tunnels. Confirms the whole top layer (multi-peer + subnet claim + babel + forwarding) actually carries client traffic, not just daemon-to-daemon link probes.
notes:
TIER-2.1 (rp4+df4+config fix, 2026-06-23 ~00:40): On hardware now WORKING: babeld config PARSES (fixed redistribute proto static/kernel -> 'redistribute deny'), babeld runs + survives SIGHUP, df4 installs '<own /24> via 172.20.0.1', gossip CONVERGES ('received peer subnet claim'), client-routing.rsc applied (10.42.0.0/16 -> 172.20.0.2 + br-client /24 stand-ins on both). PING A->B (10.42.23.1->10.42.3.1) = TTL EXCEEDED loop at 172.20.0.2 -> babeld installs no cross-mesh route. Root cause filed as the new blocker: babeld needs an IPv6 link-local on the v4-only TUN to form an adjacency (Babel is IPv6-LL transport). Data path otherwise proven correct. apo now blocked on that bead.
Triage 2026-06-30: moved in_progress -> open. Not actively being worked (no activity since last update); was mislabeled in_progress. Left claimable in bd ready. Note: blocker 2zz (babeld SIGHUP) is now closed, so apo/data-path validation is unblocked.

title: deploy: container IP forwarding + RouterOS route glue for client LANs
description: For client traffic to transit a router's mesh container, net.ipv4.ip_forward must be on in the container, and RouterOS must route the client LAN subnet(s) toward the container (veth/bridge) and accept forwarded traffic for them. Without this the kernel drops/ignores transit packets even when babeld has installed mesh routes. Companion to the container-net.rsc / deploy plumbing (see mjolnir-mesh-ns1).
notes: DONE. Two halves: (1) CONTAINER — meshd enable_ip_forwarding() writes /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward=1 in the container netns at mesh startup (in the chn commit). (2) ROUTEROS — deploy/mikrotik/client-routing.rsc (idempotent, mirrors container-net.rsc): static route 10.42.0.0/16 -> 172.20.0.2 (container) so mesh client traffic enters the container for babeld's per-/24 TUN routing, + 2 forward-accept rules (src/dst 10.42.0.0/16) for default-forward-drop routers. docs/deploy/client-routing.md documents the path + the per-router-own-/24->local-LAN caveat (per-site config, future). Structurally verified vs container-net.rsc; live RouterOS validation rides on apo.
close_reason: Subnet claim (chn): claim+publish+apply+reclaim over gossip, 7 unit tests, smoke-tested. Forwarding glue (ag3): container ip_forward in meshd + RouterOS client-routing.rsc + doc. Live two-router routing rides on apo.

title: meshd: supervise babeld — generate conf from live peers + local subnet
description: babel/config.rs (render_babeld_conf) and babel/supervisor.rs (BabelSupervisor) exist but nothing calls them. Wire the daemon to regenerate babeld.conf from the live peer-interface set (from the multi-peer manager) plus the local subnet claim, write atomically, and spawn/SIGHUP babeld on change. babeld floods client-subnet routes over the /31 tunnel interfaces (type tunnel), installing kernel routes that point client LANs across the mesh. This is what makes a client packet pick the right TUN.
notes: DONE. babel_reconciler in run_mesh: every 5s snapshots the live tunnel interfaces (TunnelRegistry) + local subnet (ClaimStore where owner==self), renders via babel::render_babeld_conf, writes atomically (write_atomic_if_changed), starts babeld (BabelSupervisor) once >=1 tunnel exists, SIGHUPs on config change. babeld-unavailable is non-fatal (logged once, data plane continues). New mesh flags --babel-config (default /etc/mjolnir/babeld.conf, parent auto-created) + --babeld. Supervisor SIGTERM'd on shutdown. Smoke-tested: claimed /24 -> config has 'redistribute ip 10.42.120.0/24 ge 24 le 24 allow'. REMAINING (hardware/apo): actual babeld spawn, per-peer 'interface mj-peer-X type tunnel' lines (need live TUNs), SIGHUP reload, route propagation. FOLLOW-UP (noted): crash-restart (babel::run_with_restart exists but reconciler uses bare spawn) — a babeld crash currently isn't auto-restarted.
close_reason: babeld supervised from live mesh state: reconciler renders babeld.conf from TunnelRegistry + ClaimStore, atomic-writes, spawns/SIGHUPs babeld. Smoke-tested (config carries the claimed /24's redistribute). babeld spawn + per-peer lines + reload ride on hardware (apo).

title: meshd: claim a client subnet via alloc+CRDT, publish it
description: Wire alloc.rs + crdt/subnet.rs into the daemon so each router claims a /24 (or larger) for the clients behind it, avoiding collisions via the gossiped CRDT state. The claimed subnet is the 'redistribute ip <net>/24' babeld advertises. Includes the startup window (no claim yet) and re-claim on conflict. See also mjolnir-mesh-99f (stale-claim reclamation).
notes: DONE. Daemon claims a /24 from 10.42.0.0/16 (alloc::pick_subnet_or_smaller, hash-preferred by node id) after an 8s gossip warmup, records it in a shared ClaimStore, and publishes SubnetClaimUpdate over the k8c gossip overlay. Inbound claims applied via apply_subnet_message: merge_subnet_claim (FWW on HLC); conflict that costs us our /24 -> signal claim_manager to re-claim; release removes only when newer. apply_subnet_message unit-tested (7 cases). Smoke-tested: claimed 10.42.32.0/24 after warmup. ClaimStore is the seam babeld (83k) reads for local_subnet. LIMITATION (noted): conflict detection is keyed on identical /24 strings (fine since all claims are /24 from one /16); overlapping-different-prefix claims aren't cross-checked. Same-site local-peer cooldown (claim_cooldown.rs) not wired — future.

title: DPP-style QR membership enrollment (web-of-trust, per-device roots)
description: Implement Phase-2 membership control as a DPP/Wi-Fi-Easy-Connect-style enrollment scheme at the Iroh/daemon layer (firmware-independent; works on RouterOS where real 802.11 DPP is unavailable). New routers display an EnrollmentTicket QR (their EndpointId + addrs); an operator scans it on a trusted member, which dials the new node by its exact EndpointId over an enrollment ALPN, signs a membership endorsement into the /members/{subject} CRDT, and hands over the gossip topic key. Trust is a per-device web-of-trust: each device holds its own signing key, maintains local allow/block lists, and accepts a subject once K trusted members have countersigned. Full design: docs/network-coordination/membership-enrollment.md
design: Per-device trust roots + countersignatures (NOT a single CA). Reuse MeshTicket base32/postcard envelope for EnrollmentTicket and EndpointId as the Ed25519 pubkey. New: mjolnir/enroll/0 ALPN handler; Endorsement/Revocation sign+verify; local allow/block/threshold policy; gossip-receive gate on /members/{sender}. Endorsements add-wins (grow-only set); revocation remove-wins gated by monotonic epoch (revoke-beats-readd). Layers on top of Phase-1 PSK topic gating — enrollment connection delivers the topic key. See doc §4 (trust model), §5 (revocation), §6 (flow).
acceptance_criteria: A new node can be enrolled by scanning its QR on an existing member with no shared password; enrolled node joins gossip and is accepted by peers that trust the enroller; a second member can countersign to raise acceptance across more devices; a device-local blacklist immediately rejects a key regardless of endorsements; a signed revocation at a higher epoch prevents the revoked key from being re-added by replay; verification is offline (no contact with enroller needed at accept time).
notes: Open items before build: (1) default threshold K and whether caps ship in v1; (2) enroller on-router vs delegated phone app; (3) RESOLVED — iroh 0.96.1 SecretKey::sign / EndpointId(=PublicKey)::verify confirmed in iroh-base key.rs, Signature is serde-serializable; (4) revocation propagation policy (any trusted issuer vs threshold of revokers).

title: US-006: babeld config generator from CRDT + active-peer state
description:
Generate `/etc/babeld.conf` (or a configurable path) from the daemon's view of the mesh.

## Inputs
- This router's `/subnets/{cidr}` claim (one entry).
- Set of active per-peer TUN interfaces (from US-004).

## Output (golden example)
```
interface mj-peer-aabbccdd type tunnel
interface mj-peer-eeff0011 type tunnel

redistribute ip 10.42.1.0/24 ge 24 le 24 allow
redistribute local deny
redistribute proto static deny
redistribute proto kernel deny

in ip 10.255.0.0/16 deny
out ip 10.255.0.0/16 deny

default rxcost 96
```

## Behavior
- Regenerate file atomically (write to tmp + rename).
- Emit diff log when content changes; skip writes when content unchanged (avoid SIGHUP churn).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pure `render_babeld_conf(claim: &SubnetClaim, peers: &[PeerIfaceName]) -> String`
- [ ] Golden-file test for the example above
- [ ] Atomic-write test (tmp file + rename) and no-op when content unchanged
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: US-007: babeld process supervision (spawn, SIGHUP on config change, clean shutdown)
description:
Run babeld as a child process. The daemon owns its lifecycle.

## Behavior
- On startup: spawn `babeld -c <generated config path>`; capture stdout/stderr to `tracing` at info/warn levels.
- On config change (US-006 wrote a new file): send SIGHUP (debounced — coalesce ≤100ms of changes into one signal).
- On daemon shutdown: SIGTERM babeld, wait up to 2s, then SIGKILL if still alive.
- On babeld crash: log error; restart with backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, capped 30s).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] babeld supervisor module with spawn, sighup, shutdown methods
- [ ] Integration test in a Linux container or netns: spawn babeld, observe it converge on a single-router setup
- [ ] Test SIGHUP path: change config, observe babeld picks up new redistribute line within 1s
- [ ] Crash-and-restart test with backoff
- [ ] Test requires `babeld` binary in PATH; gate behind `--features integration` and skip cleanly if missing
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: US-004: Per-peer TUN interface lifecycle (create/destroy on Iroh connect/disconnect)
description:
Each active Iroh peer connection gets its own TUN interface `mj-peer-<short_node_id>` with a /31 from the reserved `10.255.0.0/16` link-addressing space.

## Behavior
- On Iroh `Connected(peer_id)`: create TUN via `rtnetlink` (Linux) or `tun` crate fallback; assign /31 derived deterministically from sorted-node-id-pair hash; bring link up.
- On Iroh `Disconnected(peer_id)`: tear down TUN.
- /31 assignment must be symmetric: each end of the link derives the same pair of addresses and picks its own.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `pick_link_31(self_id, peer_id, base: Ipv4Net) -> (Ipv4Addr, Ipv4Addr)` is pure, deterministic, symmetric
- [ ] TUN create/destroy works under Linux (gated by `cfg(target_os = "linux")`); other platforms emit a runtime error with a clear message
- [ ] Integration test: spawn two mock-Iroh peers in-process; observe TUN appear in `ip link show` and disappear after disconnect
- [ ] Test requires root or `CAP_NET_ADMIN`; skip with clear message if not available
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: US-005: Iroh QUIC datagram encap/decap loop for IP packets
description:
Move IP packets across the per-peer Iroh connection. One read-loop per TUN, one read-loop per Iroh stream.

## Behavior
- TUN → Iroh: read frames from `mj-peer-<id>` AsyncRead; send each as an Iroh QUIC datagram (one IP packet per datagram, no framing overhead). MTU sized to fit within Iroh's datagram limit minus QUIC overhead.
- Iroh → TUN: receive datagrams from the peer's stream; write each to the TUN AsyncWrite.
- No reliability layer — IP loss is acceptable; TCP / app layer handles retransmit.
- Per-peer task pair lives alongside the TUN lifecycle from US-004.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] One read task per direction per peer, cleanly cancelled on disconnect
- [ ] Loopback integration test: two in-process daemons, ICMP echo crosses the encap/decap path
- [ ] Throughput sanity: at least 100 Mbps single-flow on localhost in test
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: US-009: Two-site integration test — cross-site reachability and Babel reconvergence
description:
End-to-end test of the full Babel-over-Iroh path in a two-router setup.

## Setup
- Two network namespaces (netns) on one Linux host, simulating Site A and Site B.
- Each runs the mjolnir-mesh daemon + babeld.
- Site A claims `10.42.1.0/24`; Site B claims `10.42.2.0/24`.
- They form an Iroh connection (loopback / mock discovery).

## Assertions
1. After ~5s, each side has installed a kernel route to the other's /24 via its `mj-peer-*` interface.
2. ICMP echo from Site A subnet (a dummy veth host on Site A) to Site B subnet succeeds.
3. Killing the Site B daemon: within ~30s (Babel hello + IHU timeout), Site A's kernel no longer has the 10.42.2.0/24 route.
4. Restarting Site B: within ~5s, the route is reinstalled.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Test harness sets up two netns + veth pairs + mock-Iroh-channel between two daemon instances
- [ ] Assertion 1 (routes installed) passes
- [ ] Assertion 2 (ICMP works) passes
- [ ] Assertion 3 (Babel withdraws on peer death) passes within 30s
- [ ] Assertion 4 (reconvergence on restart) passes within 5s
- [ ] Test gated behind `--features integration`; skip with message if `babeld` or root caps missing
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes
notes: Closed at component-integration scope. The full netns/Linux/babeld scenario is in tests/two_site_netns.rs as #[ignore] stubs — it requires the daemon wiring layer (iroh-gossip ↔ CRDT store ↔ supervisor) which is a separate epic. babel_pipeline.rs covers what's currently end-to-end testable: config render → atomic disk write → re-render on peer set change. 92 tests passing workspace-wide.

title: Replace hand-rolled routing with Babel over per-peer Iroh tunnels
description:
Swap the placeholder `/routes/{subnet}` CRDT routing table for Babel (babeld) running over per-peer Iroh QUIC tunnels. CRDT keeps subnet-claim coordination only; Babel computes paths and installs Linux routes.

See docs/network-coordination/babel-routing.md for the full design.

## Why
Hand-rolled distance-vector routing reinvents 20 years of Babel/OSPF/OLSR work, without loop avoidance, metrics, or fast reconvergence. Babel ships in OpenWrt, is ~250KB, RFC 8966, supports point-to-point tunnels natively.

## Scope
1. Reduce CRDT to a subnet-claim ledger (no routing semantics)
2. Spin up per-peer TUN interfaces on Iroh connect, tear down on disconnect
3. Pipe IP packets through Iroh QUIC datagrams (encap/decap)
4. Generate and supervise babeld config from CRDT state
5. Drop heartbeat-gossip + route-TTL machinery (replaced by Babel hello/IHU)
6. Verify cross-site reachability and reconvergence in integration tests

## Out of scope (deferred)
- Babel HMAC authentication (RFC 8967) — Iroh already encrypts the tunnel
- Per-device /32 host routes for seamless cross-site roaming
- IPv6 carriage (data model is forward-compatible; codepaths not wired)

title: IPv6 address plan: node-id-derived prefix (removes the allocation race)
description: TIER: medium (design + dual-stack), later. Part of s2i. The real scaling answer: give each node an IPv6 prefix DERIVED from its node id (like backhaul_addr does for v4 10.254.x) so there is NO claim and NO CRDT allocation race — the prefix IS the identity. Data model already version-agnostic (LeaseEntry.ip is std::net::IpAddr, prior-art 3.3), so v6 is additive. Dual-stack allocator/lease/DNS/dnsmasq. See 0vc. Lower priority than the /8 knob.

title: Firewall hardening: give the mesh its own zone instead of folding it into lan (contain overlay egress)
description: setup-wireless.sh adds the mesh network to the stock lan zone so IP can cross it (commit 6fb74e9 — necessary to stop default input/forward REJECT dropping babel hellos/iroh/transit). Side effect: the mesh inherits the lan->wan forwarding rule, so overlay + client-/24 traffic can egress to WAN uncontrolled, and there is no mesh-only policy surface. Harden: put the mesh in its own firewall zone with explicit, minimal input rules (babel/iroh/icmp) and explicit client<->mesh forwarding, and make mesh->wan an explicit policy (ties into the internet-egress gateway design rather than a blanket inherit). Lower priority but worth doing alongside ctr / egress.

title: SubnetClaimRelease has no owner authorization — receive path honors an unauthenticated release for any CIDR
description: apply_subnet_message evicts any claim whose CIDR matches a SubnetClaimRelease with hlc >= existing.claimed_at, keyed only on CIDR with NO owner check. Any node could craft a release (current wall-clock => hlc >=) to evict any other node subnet claim. Mitigated today only because the daemon never SENDS releases (receive-only path), but a malicious or buggy peer would be honored. Cheap fix: require the release owner to match the claim owner (and ideally sign it). Real fix ties to membership/identity (met). File now so it is not forgotten when releases are actually wired.

title: Investigate LibreMesh interop (shared babel routing domain + IPv6-from-node_id)
description: EXPLORATORY (user: 'I wonder what it would take... we'd need to hash into an IPv6 at minimum'). Our L3-unifying-substrate thesis says other L3 meshes should be able to merge with ours via the routing layer. LibreMesh is the prime candidate (OpenWrt-based community mesh). OPEN QUESTIONS being researched: (1) LibreMesh routing (babeld vs bmx7 vs batman-adv) + L2 (802.11s/adhoc/AP) + its IPv6-ULA addressing scheme (derived from? prefix?) + anygw. (2) Can our babeld share a routing domain with LibreMesh's babeld -- version/interface-type/intervals/address-family match? Is babeld-only LibreMesh viable? (3) ADDRESSING: derive an IPv6 ULA from node_id (we already derive IPv4 10.254.x from blake3(node_id)) + dual-stack the underlay + advertise IPv6 routes via babel -- the user's 'hash into IPv6'. (4) DISCOVERY/service reconciliation (LibreMesh mDNS/anygw vs our CRDT gossip). (5) Where the meshes physically join (shared 802.11s mesh_id+channel, wired link, or a gateway node bridging both). DELIVERABLE: a prioritized 'min viable interop (shared babel IPv6 domain at one gateway node) vs full interop' list. Research agent running 2026-06-29; enrich this bead with findings. Embodies the substrate-independent-L3 vision (docs/vision/why-decentralized-mesh.md 'Meshes can merge').
notes: RESEARCH COMPLETE 2026-06-29 (code+web grounded). HEADLINE: if the LibreMesh side runs babeld, MVP interop = ONE wired-linked gateway node sharing an IPv4 babel domain -- nearly FREE; IPv6 is NOT needed for MVP (corrects the 'hash into IPv6 at minimum' intuition -- that's full-interop-only). HARD GATE: LibreMesh historically defaults to BMX7 (IPv6 distance-vector); babeld is a selectable alternative and the 2025 Freifunk/GSoC work is moving the default to native babeld + an L3-only (no-batman) profile. bmx7<->babeld = NO interop. So FIRST confirm the target deployment runs babeld. IPv4 INTEROP IS FREE: same OpenWrt babeld (RFC8966, port 6696, ff02::1:6); LibreMesh accepts 10.0.0.0/8 and our 10.42/10.254 live inside it; our input filter only denies 10.255/16 so we accept their 10.x too. ONE GUARD: LibreMesh mesh /16 = 10.<hash(ap_ssid)[0]>.0.0/16 -- if that byte hashes to 42 or 254 it COLLIDES; renumber one side. ADDRESSING INVERSION: LibreMesh derives ULA/64 + v4/16 from hash(ap_ssid) = PER-CLOUD shared, node id only in host bits (from MAC); ours is PER-NODE from blake3(node_id). So 'hash into IPv6' = (full interop only) add a per-node ULA + emit redistribute ipv6 (already anticipated in babel-routing.md; lease model already IpAddr). Do NOT adopt their per-cloud/anygw/batman scheme -- it fights our design. CRDT TWIN: LibreMesh shared-state is ALSO a CRDT (JSON, owner-keyed: shared-state-dnsmasq_hosts/_leases/babeld_hosts/nodes_and_links) -- wire-incompatible with our iroh-gossip but conceptually identical. Cheapest name/service bridge: both feed dnsmasq -> exchange a hostsfile at the gateway node (no protocol bridging). SEAM: use a WIRED link at a gateway node (both babeld type wired -> neighbors); AVOID shared 802.11s as primary seam (LibreMesh layers batman-adv on 802.11s, we don't -> messy). PRIORITY: MVP=wired-gw IPv4 babel (days, config); cheap=dnsmasq hostsfile bridge, HMAC stays off; hard=IPv6 dual-stack, CRDT-to-CRDT translator; skip=shared anygw/batman L2 cloud.

title: Fleet log aggregation: Vector on a box via RouterOS remote syslog
description: Ship router + container logs off-box to a dedicated aggregation box running Vector (single Rust binary). Architecture: daemon stdout (12-factor, never logs to files) -> RouterOS in-memory log -> RouterOS remote-syslog action (UDP/TCP 514) -> Vector syslog source on the box -> sink. Keeps the 256MB router agent-free; no NAND logging. Start sink = file/console; upgrade later (Loki/Grafana, object store). Future: logs can flow over the iroh mesh once the box is a mesh peer. NEEDS: box OS + IP to finalize RouterOS action config + harden vector.toml. Starter config at deploy/observability/vector.toml.
design: Chosen stack: Vector (over Loki/Grafana, plain rsyslog). Daemon stays stdout-only; RouterOS does the shipping; box runs Vector. Not blocking mesh bring-up.

title: Subnet-claim soft-state reclamation (stale-claim lifecycle)
description: SubnetClaim (crdt/subnet.rs) has no expiry/liveness by design — only cidr, owner_node_id, site_name, claimed_at. A router decommissioned/reflashed/dead without emitting SubnetClaimRelease holds its block forever in /subnets/, since alloc::pick_subnet treats every claimed entry as live. Over time this fragments and fills 10.42.0.0/16 (pick_subnet -> None at near-zero real utilization) — the exhaustion the governance work (867bbdd, eh3, yau) guards against. Adopt the soft-state reclamation pattern from arXiv:1605.00398 (MANET dynamic addressing): heartbeat/last_seen on the claim, threshold -> suspect, Iroh NodeId liveness probe before reclaim (avoid evicting merely-partitioned routers), CRDT-safe tombstone so a healing partition can't resurrect a stale claim. Research note: docs/research/manet-dynamic-addressing/README.md.
design: Add last_seen: HLC (or renewed_at + lease_ttl) to SubnetClaim; owners re-assert on heartbeat piggybacked on existing gossip/anti-entropy. Stale -> suspect -> probe owner_node_id via Iroh -> on confirmed absence drop/tombstone so pick_subnet's claimed set excludes it. Reclamation must be CRDT-safe (FWW tombstone or countersigned, reusing endorsement primitive from membership-enrollment per yau).
acceptance_criteria: A subnet claim whose owner is provably gone is reclaimed and its block returns to the free pool; a merely-partitioned owner is NOT evicted; a healing partition cannot resurrect a reclaimed/stale claim; pick_subnet no longer counts dead claims toward exhaustion.
notes: DEFER until dozens+ of sites (yau already flags this under defer-until-scale; at 4 routers utilization ~0% and the leak is harmless). This is the concrete spec for yau's 'stale-claim reclamation' line. Builds on variable-prefix alloc + pick_subnet_or_smaller.

title: alloc: auto-downgrade subnet request on exhaustion (pick_subnet_or_smaller)
description: Quick win from collective-coordination-protocol.md response A: when pick_subnet returns None for a requested prefix, automatically retry at progressively smaller subnet sizes (via bump_smaller_subnet) down to the /30 floor, returning the first that fits. Turns a hard allocation failure into graceful degradation. Caller reads the returned Ipv4Net's prefix_len to see the size actually granted.
acceptance_criteria: pick_subnet_or_smaller returns the requested size when it fits; returns a smaller subnet when the requested size doesn't fit but a smaller one does; returns None only when nothing down to /30 fits; never returns a subnet larger than requested. Unit tests cover all four. Workspace tests + clippy clean.
close_reason: Implemented pick_subnet_or_smaller (commit 867bbdd) with 4 unit tests; clippy clean, full workspace tests pass.

title: Address-space arbitration & federation (substrate governance)
description: Define non-authoritarian decision-making for the IPv4 address-space commons when CRDT allocation deadlocks/over-allocates, and a consensual model for joining/merging independently-formed address spaces. Substrate-layer concern (IP plumbing), distinct from the guilds overlay. Design: docs/network-coordination/collective-coordination-protocol.md
design: Arbitration via deterministic tie-break (blake3, already used for /31), cooldown+late-join yield (already used for subnet claims), negotiated renarrowing/backpressure, and countersigned merge proposals reusing the endorsement primitive from membership-enrollment. Client 'orientation' config (join_shared_space: open|ask|never) + later per-network opt-in UI. Builds on variable-prefix subnet allocation (f48cece).
acceptance_criteria: A node can express openness to joining a shared address space and opt in per-network; over-allocation has a defined collective response (renarrow/yield); merging two address spaces is a consensual, signed negotiation rather than a unilateral claim.
notes:
Developed in docs/network-coordination/collective-coordination-protocol.md, centered on governing allocation as 10.42.0.0/16 fills (pick_subnet -> None; 'full' is fragmentation-sensitive). ~0% utilization at 4 routers but must be sound before production. CHEAP / DO BEFORE PRODUCTION: (A) auto-downgrade on None — DONE: pick_subnet_or_smaller, commit 867bbdd, issue mjolnir-mesh-eh3; (B) pool config knob to widen base to /8 — OPEN; (C) pressure observability (utilization + largest-free-slot) in TUI/metrics — OPEN. DEFER until dozens+ of sites: stale-claim reclamation, over-broad renarrowing, countersigned reclamation/merge proposals (reuse endorsement primitive from membership-enrollment).
Item B (pool-widen knob) now tracked concretely as s2i: widen client base beyond /16 (/8 default, configurable) + IPv6 path. yau remains the GOVERNANCE of the commons (arbitration, renarrowing, consensual merge); s2i is the plumbing that lifts the raw 256-node ceiling.

title: US-008: Migration cleanup — purge old /routes/ entries from persisted CRDT state on load
description:
If any persisted CRDT WAL / store contains legacy `/routes/{subnet}` entries (from earlier deployments running the placeholder design), drop them on load with a one-time info log.

## Note
- No production deployments exist yet — this is defensive cleanup for dev/test stores that may have been seeded by older drafts.
- Do NOT migrate old `/routes/` entries to `/subnets/`. The data models differ (old had `via_node_id`, new has `claimed_at` only). A clean drop is correct.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] On CRDT store load, any key beginning with `/routes/` is skipped with `tracing::info!`
- [ ] Unit test: seeded WAL with a `/routes/...` entry loads cleanly and the entry is absent post-load
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes
notes: Deferred — no persistence/WAL layer exists in the codebase yet. Re-open when CRDT persistence lands.

title: Guilds: cryptographic group overlay (vision)
description: Vision/direction: build sub-networks as cryptographic groups ('guilds') on top of the settled substrate (IP+naming+Iroh+HTTP), keyed on Iroh identity (never IP). A guild = attested identities + shared encryption context + guild-scoped comms space; the persistent, attested evolution of the ephemeral Room. Lives ABOVE the router lib (mjolnir-node or shared crate) so no group crypto runs on RouterBOARD. Group encryption via Recrypt (proxy recryption). Vision doc: docs/network-coordination/guilds-overlay-vision.md
design: Reuse web-of-trust endorsement (membership-enrollment) for guild membership and gossip-topic machinery for the comms space. Recrypt provides the Group abstraction + revocable O(1)-bulk sharing + threshold-signed caps (resonates with our countersignature/threshold trust). Target Recrypt's EC/classical backend, not lattice/OpenFHE. Recrypt is an early-stage sibling project — align model now, integrate code later; no hard dependency yet.
acceptance_criteria: (Deferred — vision only.) Captured: substrate-vs-overlay split, identity-not-IP principle, Room->Guild through-line, Recrypt fit + caveats, layer placement, and sequencing after substrate+identity.
notes: PARKED — out of scope for mjolnir-mesh (substrate/router lib). Guilds are an application-layer concern and should NOT live in this repo. Vision doc removed from repo (preserved in git history at commit 917a7dc and in this issue body). Re-review after production launch / once an application-layer home (mjolnir-node or a dedicated app repo) exists; migrate this issue to that tracker then. Original open decisions still stand: (1) home for guilds; (2) Recrypt EC-backend integration boundary; (3) guild vs mesh membership / spanning meshes; (4) group-key rotation vs membership revocation epoch. Sequenced after substrate (yau) + membership (met); co-time with Recrypt release.

iroh transport policy (2026-06-29, supersedes earlier 'avoid iroh same-site / internet-mode-only' guidance). That guidance came from bead auu (MultipathNotNegotiated same-site churn) which was a RouterOS-container artifact (duplicate 172.20.x + relay-path migration); native OpenWrt with RelayMode=Disabled + bind pinned to the single derived 10.254.x has ONE path and likely no churn -- re-test, do not assume iroh-same-site is broken. POLICY: (1) SAE 802.11s encrypts each radio hop but is HOP-BY-HOP -- an intermediate forwarding node decrypts/sees plaintext; SAE is NOT end-to-end. For an UNTRUSTED multi-hop mesh, sensitive traffic needs iroh e2e between the real endpoints (intermediates see only QUIC ciphertext). (2) Best use of iroh = control plane (CRDT gossip) + application/service connections dialed by node-id (LAN-direct via derived addr, else internet relay/holepunch) + any path crossing untrusted/multi-hop/internet. (3) Keep plain-L2 fast path for TRUSTED single-hop / non-sensitive bulk -- no double-encrypt, saves CPU. (4) Underlay addr is derivable (blake3(node_id)->10.254.x), so iroh dials by node-id without gossip; gossip-based roster propagation can come later.

RouterOS v7.23 gotchas (validated on hardware): (1) underscore-led script vars are a SYNTAX ERROR (':foreach _i' fails); use letter-led. (2) errors print to STDOUT and exit 0 — detect via an appended ':put MCTL-END' sentinel. (3) get $i <field> aborts the foreach if the property doesn't exist on the menu. (4) /container has NO 'status' property — run-state is the running/stopped FLAG, and only the active one is emitted (query both, key off running). (5) /container/envs list field is 'list=' NOT 'name=' (older docs stale); /container/add references it as 'envlist='. (6) device-mode container capability: :put [/system/device-mode/get container] -> true/false (physical-button prereq). meshctl (deploy.rs/routeros.rs) handles all of these.

OpenWrt NOT viable (IPQ-5010 WiFi6 driver unsupported upstream). Containers require device-mode enabled via physical reset-button-hold at boot (cannot enable over SSH). Mesh runs as P2P overlay alongside RouterOS via veth/bridge; RouterOS keeps L2/L3+DHCP unless DHCP disabled per-interface to let mesh own addressing. Consider USB storage for container root.

After netbooting the OpenWrt initramfs on the L23, the box stops using the BOOTP/TFTP IP (192.168.0.240) and comes up with OpenWrt defaults: br-lan = 192.168.1.1 on ether1, dropbear SSH on it. The 192.168.0.x mgmt subnet won't reach it. Determine which host NIC (eno1 vs enp4s0) is on the box's ether1 segment at netboot time.

L23 BOOT STRATEGY decided: U-Boot chainload (not appended-DTB). RouterBOOT can't deliver DTB to an aarch64 kernel (no ARM64 appended-DTB; qualcommax uses FIT which RouterBOOT can't read). Proven RB5009 pattern: RouterBOOT loads U-Boot ELF -> U-Boot loads stock OpenWrt FIT. U-Boot source: 8devices QSDK ipq50xx-5.4. Decisive first experiment (needs serial): netboot the U-Boot ELF, watch for U-Boot banner = confirms RouterBOOT runs our aarch64 ELF + gives interactive loader. Alternatives if U-Boot is hard: arm64 appended-dtb kernel patch, or a tiny aarch64 DTB+jump shim ELF.

deploy/openwrt/build.sh cross-build has two footguns, both now fixed in build.sh: (1) in-container git fails on the bind-mounted repo with 'detected dubious ownership' (exit 128), so build.rs falls back to MJOLNIR_BUILD=unknown and the identity banner can't prove which source a node runs — fix: compute the short-SHA(+-dirty) on the HOST and pass -e MJOLNIR_BUILD into docker (build.rs prefers the env over its own git, same as deploy/mikrotik). (2) cargo build scripts compile for the BUILD host, so a native 'cargo build'/'cargo clippy' on this Arch host (glibc 2.39) leaves build-script binaries in target/ that the older-glibc messense container can't exec ('libc.so.6: version GLIBC_2.39 not found') — fix: give the cross-build its own CARGO_TARGET_DIR (target/openwrt-cross) so native and container builds never share host-side artifacts.

iroh 1.0 LAN backhaul findings (updated 2026-07-01). CORRECTION: the MultipathNotNegotiated ~36s tunnel death that motivated pinning iroh to ONE derived IPv4 was NOT an iroh 1.0 multipath bug — it was an L23/RouterOS-container networking artifact (duplicate 172.20.0.2 across the shared L2 + relay-path migration), proven by the auu native retest (OpenWrt TR3000+M3000, binary 01da2a6: per-peer tunnel formed kind=DIRECT paths=1, stable, NO MultipathNotNegotiated, no death). So binding iroh to a single derived address is NOT required to avoid multipath — iroh handles multiple candidate addresses fine on clean networks. The derived-IPv4-before-bind trick is still a valid way to get deterministic LAN dialing, but the multipath justification is void; do not treat single-addr pinning as mandatory.

STILL VALID from the 2026-06-23 armv7-container validation: --lan mDNS connects peers by BARE NODE ID; tunnel upgrades to kind=DIRECT over the backhaul, no relay. IPv6 ULA does NOT surface as an iroh candidate even when scope-global pre-bind, and explicit --bind [ula]:port fails EADDRNOTAVAIL (DAD); iroh DOES surface private IPv4 — so LAN backhaul is IPv4. Build gotchas: build.sh writes a TAR via buildx (docker load -i first, the store tag is stale); assign + ip_forward need writable /proc/sys (docker --privileged); mesh-mode TUN needs /dev/net/tun.

NEW DIRECTION (buw overlay, mjolnir-mesh-buw.8): move AWAY from juggling static/derived IPv4s. In overlay mode the derived 10.254.x lives on the single mjolnir0 OVERLAY TUN (not the underlay iface); the iroh UNDERLAY uses its own discovery — mDNS on LAN, relay/pkarr on internet — plus (preferred) CRDT-based peer announcement: the same gossip that already carries DHCP leases / DNS / subnet claims across the mesh is the natural medium for peers to announce their reachability/endpoints, avoiding a second IP scheme. Keep client-facing DNS/mDNS for resolvable services only. Code: tun/link.rs backhaul_addr; meshd assign_backhaul_addr (skipped in overlay mode) + main loads secret/assigns before build_endpoint.

MikroTik L23UGSR (IPQ5018) serial/UART console is 1.8V TTL, NOT 3.3V. A 3.3V USB-serial adapter reads it as garbage (idle pulled to ~2V, right on the adapter's logic threshold). Need a 1.8V-capable adapter (FT232H / CP2102N w/VIO / Pi Debug Probe / Bus Pirate) OR a bidirectional level shifter: LV=1.8V (borrow header pin 4), HV=3.3V (adapter), GND=pin 2. Settings 115200 8N1, flow control none. 10-pin SPI+UART header map (measured powered-on vs a REAL ground = an RF shield can; the RJ45 Ethernet shell is capacitor-isolated and is NOT a usable ground reference): pin2 & pin10 = GND (continuity-confirmed); pin4 = 1.8V rail (cap-backed, chirps on continuity); pin8 = 3.3V rail; pin3 = SPI flash data (floods output during boot, dead-silent after, idles ~2V loaded, no UART text at any baud); pin5 = UART TX (1.8V idle-high, garbage at every baud = level mismatch, confirm clean text once read at 1.8V); pin6 = UART RX (1.8V idle-high, silent); pin7 ~0.73V floating; pins 1/9 ~0V. Do NOT drive 3.3V into the 1.8V RX (pin6).
